does: This paper introduces SwordBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating representation steering in vision models across multiple architectures and concept removal scenarios. The core contribution is twofold: first, it provides a unified evaluation suite that moves beyond language-only tasks to systematically assess steering interventions on image representations at inference time. Second, it proposes novel evaluation metrics that capture second-order effects of orthogonalization among concept activation vectors. Specifically, cross-concept robustness measures whether concept detection remains stable when inputs are orthogonalized against alternative concepts, while collateral damage quantifies unintended performance degradation on downstream tasks for inputs lacking the targeted bias. The empirical findings reveal that while linear support vector machines achieve superior separability and orthogonality properties, they fail to achieve zero collateral damage and often underperform sparse autoencoders in practical steering scenarios.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in mechanistic interpretability and representation steering, extending prior research that has primarily focused on language models toward vision domains. It responds to limitations in existing evaluation frameworks that rely on ambiguous language modeling benchmarks by creating domain-specific steering evaluation protocols. The paper connects to earlier work on concept activation vectors and orthogonal intervention methods, while introducing a more nuanced evaluation perspective that goes beyond standard separability metrics. It engages with the broader debate around different representation extraction methods—comparing approaches like linear probes, support vector machines, and sparse autoencoders—within the steering and interpretability community. The emphasis on measuring collateral damage and cross-concept robustness reflects growing awareness in the field that steering interventions must be evaluated for unintended side effects and their stability across multiple concept manipulations.
enables: This benchmark and evaluation framework enable more rigorous development of representation steering methods for vision models, establishing standards that future work must meet. The introduction of cross-concept robustness and collateral damage metrics provides concrete targets for improving steering approaches that earlier work did not systematically measure, potentially spurring algorithmic innovations that achieve both orthogonality and safe intervention properties. SwordBench facilitates comparative analysis across different model architectures and steering mechanisms, enabling researchers to identify which approaches generalize and which fail in specific conditions. The findings that sparse autoencoders outperform more interpretable linear methods in collateral damage open new research directions into understanding why certain representation extraction methods preserve task performance better. The framework also sets groundwork for evaluating steering safety in applied contexts, supporting development of AI systems where correcting biased representations can be done without degrading legitimate model capabilities.
